caveats: a: The closest public occupation mix is four-digit NAICS 722300 and includes food-service contractors and mobile food services.
a: Task exposure is researcher-mapped rather than directly measured, and no source reports wage-weighted AI exposure for caterers.
a: The BLS occupation profile is May 2023, while O*NET task descriptions were accessed in 2026.
rho: The technology survey covers restaurants rather than caterers and reports plans, not completed labor substitution.
rho: Planned spending can exceed realized implementation, while five-year product improvements are uncertain.
rho: Implementation varies sharply with firm scale, software stack, menu complexity, and event customization.
e: No public dataset separates recurring-channel caterers from one-off consumer-event firms within 722320.
e: Eligibility is estimated after the frozen LMM firm-count bridge and does not revise that dataset input.
e: A firm may have many events but still depend on a founder's personal reputation and relationships.
s5: Owner-age evidence is broad across US employer businesses and is not industry-specific.
s5: BizBuySell restaurant transactions are platform-listed small businesses, not a complete caterer or LMM control-transfer census.
s5: Retirement intention, timing, transferability, and successful closing are distinct and are not observed jointly.
q: The GAO case is federal emergency catering and may not represent corporate or social-event contracting.
q: No source directly measures five-year pass-through of AI-enabled catering cost savings.
q: Customer mix, local competition, food inflation, and venue exclusivity can materially change retention.
d5: Employment is a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service quantity and embeds productivity assumptions.
d5: NAICS 722300 includes food-service contractors and mobile food services in addition to caterers.
d5: Catering demand is exposed to recessions, event cancellations, remote-work patterns, and changes in wedding and corporate-event budgets.
o: The FDA Food Code is a model adopted and modified by jurisdictions, not a single uniform federal operating mandate.
o: O*NET's institutional-cook occupation is not specific to event caterers.
o: Drop-off formats and standardized corporate meals face more substitution than full-service, customized events.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External corporate, nonprofit, public-sector, venue, planner, and household customers usually buy a defined event package or per-guest service. Revenue is project-like at the event level; repeatability comes from recurring customer events, venue and planner referrals, and framework or preferred-vendor relationships.
lens.excluded_activities: Food-service contractors operating cafeterias or concessions under NAICS 722310, mobile food services under NAICS 722330, restaurants whose catering is incidental, captive in-house event teams, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US catering firms in the lower-middle-market band that prepare, deliver, and often serve food for customer events, with repeat demand from corporate clients, institutions, venues, event planners, or recurring social-event referral channels.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation and wage mix for NAICS 722300 Special Food Services, not 722320 alone; O*NET describes tasks for food-service managers and institutional cooks rather than measuring AI exposure. I mapped the BLS wage mix to those task descriptions, assigning greater exposure to quoting, scheduling, reservations, inventory, purchasing, payroll, records, and marketing, and little exposure to cooking, setup, transport, serving, cleaning, and on-site supervision. Inclusion of contract food service in NAICS 722300 likely overweights physical institutional work and biases the estimate downward for event caterers, while treating structured administrative tasks as substitutable may bias it upward where exceptions and customer interaction dominate.
caveats: The closest public occupation mix is four-digit NAICS 722300 and includes food-service contractors and mobile food services.
Task exposure is researcher-mapped rather than directly measured, and no source reports wage-weighted AI exposure for caterers.
The BLS occupation profile is May 2023, while O*NET task descriptions were accessed in 2026.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 722320 staffing pattern with occupation wages, plus a time-and-motion study of caterer quoting, event planning, scheduling, purchasing, production, delivery, and service tasks tagged for current automation status, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: In the BLS 2023 Special Food Services profile, food-preparation and serving occupations were 73.32% of employment, compared with 4.67% management and 1.69% business and financial operations. The latter groups earn more and contain document-heavy work, so their wage contribution is larger than their headcount. O*NET shows automatable components such as scheduling, inventory records, budgeting, reservations, menu planning, and supply estimation, but the dominant cooking and service tasks remain physical. The base therefore represents a meaningful but minority wage-weighted task surface.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in NAICS 722300 Special Food Services from 778,300 in 2024 to 822,100 in 2034, a 5.6% ten-year increase, but does not publish six-digit 722320 real output. I annualized that path and applied five years, yielding about 1.03 as a quantity anchor, then widened the range for productivity, mix, and cycle effects. Employment may understate demand if productivity rises, but inclusion of food-service contractors and mobile services can overstate or understate caterer demand depending on segment growth.
caveats: Employment is a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service quantity and embeds productivity assumptions.
NAICS 722300 includes food-service contractors and mobile food services in addition to caterers.
Catering demand is exposed to recessions, event cancellations, remote-work patterns, and changes in wedding and corporate-event budgets.
d5.change_evidence: A BLS or Census six-digit 722320 constant-dollar output series and forecast, decomposed by corporate, social, public-sector, and venue-linked events, would materially change this estimate; event-count and guest-count panels from catering platforms would be a practical alternative.
d5.rationale: The closest official industry projection implies modest growth rather than structural decline. Corporate events, weddings, nonprofit functions, and public events remain cyclical and discretionary, while outsourcing convenience and population growth support demand. Holding price and quality constant, the base follows the BLS special-food-services employment path rather than nominal catering-market forecasts.

caveats: No public dataset separates recurring-channel caterers from one-off consumer-event firms within 722320.
Eligibility is estimated after the frozen LMM firm-count bridge and does not revise that dataset input.
A firm may have many events but still depend on a founder's personal reputation and relationships.
e.change_evidence: A stratified sample of LMM 722320 firms showing revenue by repeat customer, venue or planner referral, customer concentration, owner-produced sales, permit transferability, and second-layer management would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: NAICS 722320 consists of single-event food-service establishments, so all firms serve external event demand but not all have a repeatable customer engine. The base assumes a majority of LMM firms have recurring corporate, venue, planner, nonprofit, or public-sector channels and transferable staff, permits, vendor relationships, and operating processes. Purely owner-led bespoke firms, heavily household-wedding-dependent firms without repeat channels, and firms whose economics depend on nontransferable owner relationships are ineligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 722320 ($5,702,505,295) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 722320 ($13,676,871,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1947 (margins.json[72]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Restaurant/Dining EBITDA/Sales 19.47%); revenue/employee $100,465 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 722320); class <5: 7,096 firms, avg revenue/firm $302,713 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,938 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,177 firms, avg revenue/firm $712,362 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $138,697 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,633 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,206,441 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $234,894 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,561 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,219,315 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $626,801 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 211 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,506,047 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,850,827 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 55 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,759,327 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,457,741 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 266

caveats: The FDA Food Code is a model adopted and modified by jurisdictions, not a single uniform federal operating mandate.
O*NET's institutional-cook occupation is not specific to event caterers.
Drop-off formats and standardized corporate meals face more substitution than full-service, customized events.
o.change_evidence: A customer-level substitution study tracking catered events that moved to self-service, restaurant delivery, automated production, or software-only coordination, together with state permit and person-in-charge treatment of those models, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Most of the year-five catering quantity still requires an accountable operator to procure and transport food, prepare it safely, coordinate timing, manage staff, set up, serve or hand off, clean, and resolve on-site exceptions. O*NET describes institutional-cook duties dominated by cooking, temperature monitoring, storage, cleaning, serving, and inventory, while FDA identifies the Food Code as the regulatory model for retail and food service. Software can remove ordering and administrative touches, and some customers can self-serve or shift to restaurants and delivery platforms, but it cannot eliminate responsibility for physical execution and food safety for most catered events.

caveats: The GAO case is federal emergency catering and may not represent corporate or social-event contracting.
No source directly measures five-year pass-through of AI-enabled catering cost savings.
Customer mix, local competition, food inflation, and venue exclusivity can materially change retention.
q.change_evidence: A contract-level panel from caterers showing pricing method, term, escalation clauses, rebid frequency, win rates, gross-margin changes after automation, and customer-specific renewal prices would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Event catering is commonly quoted as a fixed package or per-person price before performance. GAO's catering decision illustrates a fixed-price award in which the contractor bears actual-cost risk and meal prices were evaluated per person per day. That structure lets an operator retain near-term labor savings on signed work, but event-by-event quoting, preferred-vendor renewals, competitive rebids, transparent per-guest pricing, and customer negotiation share savings over time. The discounted base retains more than half of implemented gross benefit while allowing substantial repricing leakage.

rho.bridge: The National Restaurant Association surveyed restaurant operators about planned technology spending, not caterers about five-year implementation of AI-exposed work. I used the reported 16% planning AI integration, 52% planning back-office technology, 37% planning automated recruitment or scheduling, and 47% expecting labor-shortage automation to become more common as adoption anchors, then allowed five years for diffusion. Restaurant respondents, especially chains, may have more standardized workflows and capital than independent caterers, biasing implementation upward; the one-year investment horizon can bias it downward relative to a five-year horizon.
caveats: The technology survey covers restaurants rather than caterers and reports plans, not completed labor substitution.
Planned spending can exceed realized implementation, while five-year product improvements are uncertain.
Implementation varies sharply with firm scale, software stack, menu complexity, and event customization.
rho.change_evidence: A representative 722320 operator survey measuring current use, production deployment, error rates, labor hours avoided, system integration, and abandonment of AI tools, followed longitudinally for five years, would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Half of the exposed task surface is expected to become operationally implemented within five years. Readily packaged tools can assist proposals, customer communications, scheduling, forecasting, purchasing, bookkeeping, and marketing, but fragmented systems, event-specific exceptions, food-safety accountability, training, data quality, and small-firm change capacity constrain full implementation. The low case reflects pilots that remain advisory; the high case reflects integrated catering-management platforms with dependable human review.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners were at least 55 in 2018, Project Equity reports almost 3 million US businesses with owners at or near retirement age and says most lack plans, and BizBuySell reports 2,516 closed restaurant transactions in 2025. None measures five-year qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM caterers. I converted broad age pressure into a five-year exit window, used observed restaurant deal flow only as evidence that food-service businesses can transact, and discounted for non-sale closures, family transfers without a control transaction, internal reorganizations, subscale listings, and owner-dependent operations. The broad owner-age statistic may overstate caterer retirement pressure, while marketplace data undercount private and larger transactions.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is broad across US employer businesses and is not industry-specific.
BizBuySell restaurant transactions are platform-listed small businesses, not a complete caterer or LMM control-transfer census.
Retirement intention, timing, transferability, and successful closing are distinct and are not observed jointly.
s5.change_evidence: Broker and quality-of-earnings data for 722320 showing annual closed control sales, failed listings, closures, seller age, EBITDA, and buyer type, matched to the population of eligible LMM firms, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: The base reflects meaningful succession pressure but recognizes that age does not imply an imminent sale and many small food businesses close rather than transfer. BizBuySell's 2025 restaurant-market median revenue of $675,788 is far below the screened EBITDA band, so its transaction count supports market existence rather than a direct rate. A qualifying transfer is therefore materially less likely than the broad share of older owners.

principal_driver: Repeat-event operators can use AI to compress high-friction quoting, planning, scheduling, purchasing, and back-office work while retaining a locally delivered physical service.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and public data do not isolate LMM caterers, leaving both the implementable task share and qualifying transfer rate dependent on broad proxies.

sources.supports: Defines 722320 as establishments primarily engaged in single event-based food services, grounding the industry boundary, external event model, eligibility estimate, and pricing discussion.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 722320 Caterers

sources.supports: Reports 720,600 total jobs; food-preparation and serving occupations at 73.32% of employment, management at 4.67%, and business and financial operations at 1.69%, with occupation wages used to anchor the wage-weighted task-exposure estimate.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 722300 Special Food Services

sources.supports: Lists catering-manager-relevant tasks including scheduling, inventory records, budgets, payroll review, purchasing, reservations, complaints, staffing, and food preparation, separating digital and physical work.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Food Service Managers

sources.supports: Lists physical and safety-critical duties such as temperature monitoring, cooking, storage, cleaning, serving, inventory, and supply requisition, supporting limited AI substitution and continued operator need.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Cooks, Institution and Cafeteria

sources.supports: Reports restaurant-operator technology plans: 52% for back-office functions, 37% for automated recruitment or scheduling, 16% for AI integration, and 47% expecting labor-shortage technology and automation to become more common.
sources.title: Restaurant Technology Landscape Report 2024

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey covering data year 2018, anchoring broad succession pressure.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Reports almost 3 million US businesses with owners at or near retirement age and states that most retirement-aged owners lack plans, supporting broad succession pressure while not measuring caterer transfers.
sources.title: Project Equity Announces Employee Ownership Accelerator Program

sources.supports: Reports 2,516 restaurant transactions in 2025 and median sold-business revenue of $675,788, evidencing food-service deal flow while showing the marketplace population is generally below the screened band.
sources.title: Restaurant Sales Found Their Footing Late in 2025

sources.supports: Projects NAICS 722300 Special Food Services employment from 778,300 in 2024 to 822,100 in 2034, a 5.6% increase, used as the closest official quantity-direction proxy.
sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry, 2024 and Projected 2034

sources.supports: Explains that the Food Code supplies the scientific and legal model used by jurisdictions to regulate retail and food service, supporting continued safety accountability and operator oversight.
sources.title: FDA Food Code

sources.supports: Documents a fixed-price catering procurement in which the contractor bears actual-cost risk and meal pricing was evaluated per person per day, supporting the value-capture contract-structure estimate.
sources.title: R&G Food Service, Inc., d/b/a Port-A-Pit Catering

title: Caterers
